courseTitle: Relations & Functions — Class XI Crash Course

modules.title: Cartesian Products of Sets
modules.hook: Every T-shirt order is a pair — (size, colour). List every possible pairing from 3 sizes and 4 colours, and you've built a Cartesian product without knowing it.
modules.definition: Given non-empty sets A and B, the Cartesian product $A \times B$ is the set of ALL ordered pairs $(a,b)$ where $a \in A$ and $b \in B$. Order matters — $(a,b)$ is a different object from $(b,a)$ unless $a=b$.
notation: $A \times B$ — Cartesian product of A and B

concepts.heading: Equality of ordered pairs
concepts.body: Two ordered pairs are equal only when BOTH coordinates match: $(a,b)=(x,y) \iff a=x \text{ and } b=y$. This is how you solve for unknowns hidden inside ordered pairs.

concepts.heading: Counting the pairs
concepts.body: If $n(A)=p$ and $n(B)=q$, then $n(A \times B) = pq$. Every element of A pairs with every element of B exactly once.

diagram.caption: A × B: 2 colours × 3 objects = 6 ordered pairs

video.caption: Coming soon: building A×B pair by pair

tricks: $A \times B \ne B \times A$ in general — order matters, just like inside the ordered pair itself.
If either A or B is $\varnothing$, then $A \times B = \varnothing$ too — nothing to pair with nothing.
$n(A \times B) = n(A) \times n(B)$ — multiply the counts, don't add them.

examples.question: $A=\{1,2,3\}$, $B=\{3,4\}$, $C=\{4,5,6\}$. Verify $A\times(B\cap C) = (A\times B)\cap(A\times C)$.
steps: $A\times B$ and $A\times C$ both contain these same three pairs among others

quiz.q: $n(A)=3$, $n(B)=4$. What is $n(A\times B)$?

feedback.0: That's $n(A)+n(B)$ — Cartesian product counts multiply, not add.

quiz.q: $A=\{1,2\}$, $B=\varnothing$. What is $A\times B$?

feedback.1: There's nothing in B to pair with — the product is empty, not equal to A.

quiz.q: Is $A\times B = B\times A$ always true?

feedback.1: Order matters in ordered pairs — swap A and B and you generally get a different set of pairs, unless A=B.
feedback.3: Equal counts doesn't mean equal pairs — e.g. A={1,2}, B={3,4} have the same size but A×B≠B×A.

quiz.q: $P=\{a,b,c\}$, $Q=\{r\}$. Find $P\times Q$.

feedback.1: That's $Q\times P$, not $P\times Q$ — the first set listed comes first in every pair.

quiz.q: $P=\{1,2\}$. How many elements does $P\times P\times P$ have?

modules.title: Relations
modules.hook: A relation is just SOME of the arrows out of a Cartesian product — not the full 'everyone could match everyone' set, but the specific pairs that actually got picked.
modules.definition: A relation R from a non-empty set A to a non-empty set B is simply a SUBSET of $A\times B$. It's built by keeping only the ordered pairs that satisfy some rule — not every possible pair has to be included.
notation: $R \subset A \times B$ — a relation from A to B
Domain — set of first elements actually used
Range — set of second elements actually used
Codomain — the entire target set B

concepts.heading: Arrow diagrams make relations visual
concepts.body: Draw set A as dots in one oval, set B as dots in another, and draw an arrow from $a$ to $b$ exactly when $(a,b)\in R$. This picture makes domain, range, and 'is it a function' questions much easier to answer.

diagram.caption: R = {(a,Ali),(a,Bhanu),(b,Binoy),(c,Chandra)} — x is the first letter of y

concepts.heading: Domain, Range, and Codomain aren't the same thing
concepts.body: **Domain** = all first elements actually appearing in R. **Range** = all second elements actually appearing in R. **Codomain** = the ENTIRE set B, whether or not every element gets used. Range is always a subset of Codomain — often a strictly smaller one.

video.caption: Coming soon: reading domain, range and codomain off an arrow diagram

tricks: Range ⊆ Codomain always — but they're only EQUAL when every element of B is somebody's image.
Total number of relations from A to B = $2^{pq}$ where $n(A)=p, n(B)=q$ — same '$2^n$ subsets' idea, just applied to $A\times B$ instead of a single set.
An element of A with NO arrow leaving it is simply not in the domain — that's allowed for relations (though not for functions, next module).

examples.question: $A=\{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$. $R=\{(x,y): y=x+1\}$. Find the domain, range and codomain.

diagram.caption: y = x+1 — note 6 has no outgoing arrow

examples.question: $P=\{9,4,25\}$, and R is 'x is the square of y'. Find the domain and range.

quiz.q: A relation R from A to B is best described as:
options: A function from A to B

quiz.q: $R=\{(1,2),(2,3),(3,4)\}$ from $A=\{1,2,3,4\}$ to itself. What is the domain?

feedback.1: Those are the SECOND elements — that's the range, not the domain.

quiz.q: Using the same R, what is the range?

feedback.0: Those are the FIRST elements — that's the domain, not the range.

quiz.q: If $n(A)=2$ and $n(B)=3$, how many relations exist from A to B?

feedback.2: That's just $n(A\times B)$ itself — the number of RELATIONS is 2 raised to that count.

quiz.q: Range is always ___ Codomain.
In an arrow diagram, an element of A with zero outgoing arrows means:
options: It's simply not in the domain — perfectly valid for a relation
It must be in the range instead

modules.title: Functions
modules.hook: A vending machine is a function: press B4, get exactly one snack — never two, never zero. A relation that sometimes gives two snacks (or none) for the same button isn't a function.
modules.definition: A function is a relation with a stricter rule: EVERY element of A must have EXACTLY ONE image in B — not zero, not two or more. If even one element of A breaks this rule, it's just a relation, not a function.
notation: $f: A \to B$ — f is a function from A to B
$f(a)=b$ — b is the image of a
a is the preimage of b

concepts.heading: Two ways a relation fails to be a function
concepts.body: **Missing image**: some element of the domain has NO arrow out (undefined for that input). **Multiple images**: some element has TWO OR MORE arrows out (ambiguous output). A function must have exactly one arrow out of every element of A — no more, no less.

concepts.heading: Real functions
concepts.body: A function is called **real-valued** if its range is $\mathbb{R}$ or a subset of $\mathbb{R}$. If its domain is also $\mathbb{R}$ or a subset of $\mathbb{R}$, it's called a **real function** — these are the ones you'll graph in the next module.

video.caption: Coming soon: spotting the difference between a relation and a function

tricks: Quick check on an arrow diagram: count arrows leaving EACH dot in A. Function ⟺ every dot has exactly one arrow out.
A relation can 'skip' an element of A (leave it with no arrow) — a function cannot.
$f(a)=b$ reads as 'a's image is b' — a is the preimage, b is the image. Don't mix the direction up.

examples.question: Is $R=\{(2,1),(3,1),(4,2)\}$ a function?
steps: Each of 2, 3, 4 has exactly one image (1, 1, 2 respectively)
Repeated OUTPUTS are fine — only repeated INPUTS with different outputs would break it
examples.answer: Yes, it's a function — every domain element has exactly one image.

examples.question: Is $R=\{(2,2),(2,4),(3,3),(4,4)\}$ a function?
steps: 2 appears as a first element twice: $(2,2)$ and $(2,4)$
Two different images for the same input — 2 maps to both 2 and 4
examples.answer: No — 2 has two images, so this is not a function.

quiz.q: Which of these is NOT a function?

feedback.1: 1 appears twice as a first element, with two different second elements (2 and 3) — that's ambiguous, not a function.

quiz.q: $f(x)=2x+1$. What is $f(3)$?
A function $f:A\to B$ requires every element of A to have:
options: At least one image, possibly more
At most one image, zero allowed

quiz.q: If $f(a)=b$, then $a$ is called the:

feedback.1: b is the image of a — a is the preimage. You've swapped the direction.

quiz.q: A real-valued function has a range that is:

options: Yes — repeated outputs are fine, inputs are all different
No — every input needs a different output

feedback.1: Only repeated INPUTS with different outputs break a function — repeated outputs are totally fine.
feedback.2: A function only requires each input to have ONE output, not that outputs are all distinct.

modules.title: Seven Functions Worth Memorizing
modules.hook: Seven function shapes show up on almost every graph question — learn the SHAPE and domain/range of each, and you can often answer without computing a single value.
modules.definition: These seven standard real functions recur constantly in board exams. Each has its own name, shape, domain and range — memorize them as a set.

concepts.heading: Identity function
concepts.body: $f(x)=x$ for every $x\in\mathbb{R}$. Domain = Range = $\mathbb{R}$. A straight line through the origin at 45°.

concepts.heading: Constant function
concepts.body: $f(x)=c$ for every $x$, where $c$ is fixed. Domain = $\mathbb{R}$, but Range = $\{c\}$ — just one value, no matter what x is.

concepts.heading: Polynomial functions
concepts.body: $f(x)=a_0+a_1x+a_2x^2+\dots+a_nx^n$. Quadratics ($x^2$) give a parabola; cubics ($x^3$) give an S-curve through the origin.

concepts.heading: Rational functions
concepts.body: A ratio of two polynomials, $\frac{f(x)}{g(x)}$, defined wherever $g(x)\ne0$. The simplest example, $f(x)=\frac{1}{x}$, is undefined at $x=0$ and splits into two separate branches.

concepts.heading: Modulus function
concepts.body: $f(x)=|x|$ — always non-negative. Domain = $\mathbb{R}$, Range = $[0,\infty)$. A 'V' shape with its vertex at the origin.

concepts.heading: Signum function
concepts.body: Outputs only $-1$, $0$, or $1$ depending on the sign of x: $1$ if $x>0$, $0$ if $x=0$, $-1$ if $x<0$. Range = $\{-1,0,1\}$ — a genuinely tiny range for an infinite domain.

diagram.caption: f(x) = |x|/x for x≠0, and 0 for x=0

concepts.heading: Greatest integer function
concepts.body: $f(x)=[x]$ rounds DOWN to the nearest integer — including for negatives, where it's easy to trip up: $[-2.3]=-3$, not $-2$. Domain = $\mathbb{R}$, Range = $\mathbb{Z}$.

video.caption: Coming soon: all seven function shapes side by side

tricks: Signum and greatest-integer are the two 'jump' functions in this list — every other one is a single smooth, unbroken curve.
$[x]$ ALWAYS rounds down, even for negatives — $[-2.3]=-3$ is the #1 trap in this topic, not $-2$.
Domain of $\frac{1}{x}$-style rational functions: exclude wherever the denominator is zero — always state it as $\mathbb{R}-\{\text{those points}\}$.

examples.question: Find the domain of $f(x)=\frac{x^2+3x+5}{x^2-5x+4}$.
steps: f is undefined wherever the denominator is 0, i.e. $x=1,4$

examples.question: $f(x)=|x|$. Find $f(-4)$ and $f(-1)$.
examples.answer: The modulus of a negative number is its positive counterpart.

quiz.q: What is $[-2.3]$?

feedback.1: Greatest integer rounds DOWN, even for negatives — -2.3 rounds down to -3, not up to -2.

quiz.q: Range of the signum function is:
Domain of $f(x)=\frac{1}{x}$ is:

feedback.1: x=0 must be excluded — division by zero is undefined.

quiz.q: Range of $f(x)=|x|$ is:

feedback.2: Modulus is never negative — the range is non-negative numbers, the opposite of this option.

quiz.q: For a constant function $f(x)=c$, the range is:
Which function's graph is a straight line through the origin at 45°?

modules.title: Algebra of Real Functions
modules.hook: You already know how to add, subtract, and multiply functions — you did it for real numbers for years. Do the exact same thing, just one x at a time.
modules.definition: Given two real functions f and g with the same domain X, you can combine them exactly like numbers — add, subtract, scale, multiply, divide — just apply the rule at each x individually (this is called doing it 'pointwise').

concepts.heading: The five operations
concepts.body: **Sum**: $(f+g)(x)=f(x)+g(x)$. **Difference**: $(f-g)(x)=f(x)-g(x)$. **Scalar multiple**: $(\alpha f)(x) = \alpha \cdot f(x)$ for a real number $\alpha$. **Product**: $(fg)(x)=f(x)\cdot g(x)$. **Quotient**: $\left(\frac{f}{g}\right)(x)=\frac{f(x)}{g(x)}$, valid only where $g(x)\ne0$.

video.caption: Coming soon: combining two functions pointwise

tricks: Scalar multiple ($\alpha f$, one function times a number) and product ($fg$, two functions multiplied together) look similar but are completely different operations — don't mix them up.
$f/g$ always needs a domain restriction: wherever $g(x)=0$ gets excluded, even if the original domain didn't exclude it.
All five operations are done POINTWISE — compute $f(x)$ and $g(x)$ separately first, then combine the numbers.

quiz.q: Using the same f, g: $(f-g)(x) = ?$

feedback.1: That's $(f+g)(x)$ — check your sign on the g(x) term.

quiz.q: $(fg)(x)$ means:
For $\left(\frac{f}{g}\right)(x)$ to be defined, we need:
$(\alpha f)(x)$ for a real number $\alpha$ means:

feedback.2: That would change the INPUT — scalar multiplication changes the OUTPUT instead.
That's just $f(2)$ alone, not the product $f(2)\cdot g(2)$.

questions.q: Domain of a relation is the set of:
A relation is a function only if every domain element has:

feedback.1: Greatest integer rounds down, even for negatives — -3.7 rounds down to -4.

questions.q: Which operation multiplies two functions together?
Total relations from A to B where $n(A)=2, n(B)=2$?

feedback.1: That's just $n(A\times B)=4$ — the relations count is $2^4=16$.
